front: What is the Halting Problem, and why is it significant?
back: The Halting Problem is the problem of determining, from a description of an arbitrary computer program and an input, whether the program will finish running (halt) or continue to run forever. It is significant because Alan Turing proved it is undecidable, meaning no algorithm exists that can solve it for all possible program-input pairs, establishing a fundamental limitation of algorithmic power.

front: What does it mean for a problem to be 'undecidable'?
back: An undecidable problem is a decision problem for which it is proven to be impossible to construct an algorithm that always leads to a correct yes-or-no answer. No Turing machine can solve the problem for all inputs. The Halting Problem is the most famous example.

The Church-Turing Thesis is a hypothesis stating that any function that is effectively computable (i.e., can be computed by an algorithm) is computable by a Turing machine. It implies that the Turing machine is a universal model of computation, defining the ultimate boundaries of what is algorithmically possible.

front: What is the difference between an intractable problem and an undecidable one?
back: An undecidable problem has no algorithm at all to solve it. An intractable problem has algorithmic solutions, but they are not efficient; they are problems for which all known algorithms require exponential or super-polynomial time (e.g., O(2^n)), making them infeasible to solve for large inputs, even though they are technically decidable.

front: What are NP-Complete problems, and why are they important in understanding limitations?
back: NP-Complete problems are a class of decision problems in NP for which no known polynomial-time algorithm exists, but a solution can be verified quickly. They are important because if a polynomial-time algorithm is found for any one NP-Complete problem, it could be adapted to solve all problems in NP efficiently. Their intractability represents a major practical limitation of algorithms for solving complex real-world problems.
